clean_story:
Numbers don’t lie. People do.

The phrase how math exposes lies comes from its role as a truth detector—like a scale that never tips for flattery. Math doesn’t care about opinions; it only measures what is.

A politician claims crime dropped 20%. But raw numbers show arrests fell because police stopped recording minor offenses—not because streets got safer. A company brags its product is "90% effective." Fine print reveals that’s 90% in lab tests with 12 people, not 90% of real users. Math strips away the words, leaving only the skeleton of fact.

Here’s how it works: Proportions betray spin. If a town says "only 5% of residents complain," but 80% of those complaints come from one neighborhood, the problem isn’t small—it’s concentrated. Averages hide extremes. A school boasts an "average" class size of 20, but half the classes have 30 kids and half have 10. The mean lies. Probability exposes luck. A "miracle" drug with a 1-in-1,000 chance of working will still succeed once if tested on 1,000 people—that’s not magic, just odds.

Tools like Benford’s Law (which predicts how often digits appear in real data) flag fake tax returns or election results when numbers defy natural patterns. In 2009, mathematicians proved Iran’s election data was likely fabricated because the digit "7" appeared too often in vote counts.

Three related subjects are statistical fallacies in media, how graphs distort truth, and why humans trust stories over data.
